content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 11, 1992


Honorable Don Rogers
Senator, Sixteenth Senatorial District
State Capitol
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-92-447

Dear Senator Rogers:

This is in reply to your request for advice under the
Political Reform Act¹ concerning the receipt of financial help
from various individuals to settle assessments against you by the
Internal Revenue Service.

Specifically, you inquire whether these donations can be made
to you and, if so, the form in which they can be made and whether
and how they must be reported.

Since each of these donations would be made to you for a
personal rather than a political purpose, it would be considered a
gift and not a political contribution.² (See Sections 82015 and
82028.) Under Section 89504, elected state officers such as
yourself are prohibited from accepting gifts in excess of $250 per
person during each calendar year. Accordingly, though it would be
permissible to accept donations to pay Internal Revenue Service
assessments against you, these donations cannot exceed $250 per
person during each calendar year. If any person making the
donation has already given you a gift during the same calendar
year, please note that the value of that gift also will be
attributed toward the $250 limitation. As long as these limits
are followed in your acceptance of the donations and the donations
are resolved as described below, there is no need to establish a
special fund for receipt of the donations.

Finally, under the Political Reform Act, all gifts you
receive from a person which total $50 or more each year must be

_________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

² Note, that you are prohibited from using political contributions
to pay your personal debts. (See Sections 89510(b) and 89512.)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-92-447
Page 2


reported on your annual statement of economic interests.  Thus,
any donations you receive to pay the Internal Revenue Service
assessments against you which, by themselves or when added to
other gifts received from the same person during the calendar
year, total to $50 or more must be reported on your annual
statement of economic interests.

I hope this has been of assistance to you.  However, if you
have any questions, please contact me at your convenience.

                                                Sincerely,

                                                [signature]
                                                Wayne Ordos
                                                Executive Director

sections.question: Whether donations to settle IRS assessments can be made to the Senator, in what form they can be made, and whether and how they must be reported.
sections.conclusion: Donations to pay IRS assessments are gifts rather than political contributions. Elected state officers are prohibited from accepting gifts exceeding $250 per person per calendar year. Donations meeting this limit may be accepted without establishing a special fund, and gifts totaling $50 or more per person per year must be reported on the annual statement of economic interests.
sections.question_synthetic: Senator Rogers asks whether he may accept financial donations from individuals to pay his personal IRS tax assessments, what form such donations may take, and what reporting requirements apply.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that such donations qualify as personal gifts (not political contributions) and are subject to a $250 per-person annual limit. Gifts of $50 or more annually from the same person must be reported on the statement of economic interests, but no special fund is required.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Clear advice letter with distinct question and conclusion sections. Document contains specific statutory citations and practical guidance on gift limits and reporting requirements. No structural ambiguities.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether donations to settle IRS assessments can be made to the Senator, in what form they can be made, and whether and how they must be reported.

CONCLUSION: Donations to pay IRS assessments are gifts rather than political contributions. Elected state officers are prohibited from accepting gifts exceeding $250 per person per calendar year. Donations meeting this limit may be accepted without establishing a special fund, and gifts totaling $50 or more per person per year must be reported on the annual statement of economic interests.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to Senator Don Rogers' inquiry about accepting financial donations to pay personal IRS tax assessments, clarifying that such donations are gifts subject to the $250 annual per-person limit and requiring reporting of gifts totaling $50 or more annually.
